title: Prompt Engineering
lesson:
A language model is a brilliant new hire who has read most of the internet but has zero idea what you want today. It cannot read your mind. The only thing it sees is your text. Prompt engineering is the craft of writing that text so the model reliably does the job you actually need. It is the steering wheel of the whole car.

Here is the mental shift most beginners miss: the model does not "understand" your request the way a person does. It predicts the next likely chunk of text given everything before it (see How AI Models Work). So a prompt is less a command and more a setup: you arrange words that make the answer you want the most probable thing to come next.

Analogy 1: ordering at a busy kitchen

Imagine shouting an order at a chef during the dinner rush. "Make me something good" gets you whatever they feel like. "Margherita pizza, extra basil, well-done crust, cut in six" gets you exactly that. Vague prompts are the first version. Specific prompts are the second. The chef is equally skilled both times; the difference is entirely in how clearly you ordered.

Analogy 2: a brief for a freelancer

If you hired a designer and said "make a logo," you would be unhappy with the result. Good clients give a brief: who it is for, the style, examples they like, what to avoid, and the format to deliver. A strong prompt is exactly that brief. The more an expert would need to know to do the job right, the more your prompt should contain.

The core levers

A few moves do most of the work:

  • Give a role. "You are a senior tax accountant" nudges the model toward that vocabulary and care level. This often lives in the System Prompts.
  • Be specific about the task and the output. Say the format you want: "reply with exactly three bullet points" or "return JSON with keys title and summary."
  • Show examples. Giving one or more worked examples is called few-shot prompting ("few-shot" = a few demonstrations in the prompt). No examples is zero-shot. Examples are the single fastest way to lock in a style or format.
  • Add constraints. Word limits, tone, "only use the text I gave you," "say I don't know if unsure."
  • Give it room to think. For tricky problems, "think step by step before answering" is chain-of-thought prompting. It lets the model lay out reasoning instead of blurting a guess. With newer Reasoning Models, this happens internally, so you instead just state the goal clearly and let them work.

A worked example

Compare a weak and a strong prompt for the same goal.

Weak:
  Summarise this email.

Strong:
  You are an executive assistant. Summarise the email below
  for a busy CEO in 2 sentences, then list any action items
  as bullets. If there are no action items, write "None".
  ---
  <email text here>

The strong version pins down the role, length, structure, and an edge case (no action items). That removes guesswork, which is where bad outputs come from.

Structure your prompt

For anything beyond a quick question, separate the fixed instructions from the data the user supplies. A clear delimiter (a line of dashes, XML-style tags, or triple backticks) tells the model where each part begins. This also reduces a real risk called prompt injection, where text inside the data tries to hijack your instructions ("ignore the above and do this instead").

prompt = f"""You are a support classifier.
Return one label: billing, technical, or other.

<ticket>
{user_message}
</ticket>

Label:"""

Notice the model is set up to continue after "Label:" with just the word, not a paragraph. You are shaping the shape of the answer, not only its content.

It is iterative

Nobody writes the perfect prompt first try. Treat it like tuning a recipe: run it, look at the failures, add the one instruction that would have fixed them, repeat. Save a handful of hard test cases and re-run your prompt against all of them after each change, so a fix for one case does not quietly break another. This loop matters more than any clever trick, and it connects directly to Human Evals. Prompt engineering is cheap, fast, and the first tool to reach for before you ever consider Fine-Tuning Basics.
mentalModel: The model is a genius with amnesia who can only see your text. Your prompt is the entire briefing they get before doing the job — so write the brief an expert would need, not a vague wish.
mistakes: Being vague and hoping. "Make this better" gives the model nothing to aim at. Spell out what "better" means: shorter? more formal? fewer jargon words? The model fills gaps with guesses, and guesses are where bad answers live.

Not specifying the output format. If you need JSON, three bullets, or a one-word label, say so explicitly. Otherwise you get prose you then have to clean up, which breaks any code that expected a fixed shape.

Telling it what NOT to do instead of what TO do. "Don't be formal" is weaker than "write like a friendly text to a colleague." Positive, concrete instructions steer far better than a pile of negatives.

Mixing instructions and data with no separator. Pasting a user's text straight after your instructions invites confusion and prompt injection. Wrap the data in clear delimiters like <ticket>...</ticket> so the model knows what is content versus command.

Cramming ten tasks into one prompt. "Translate, summarise, fact-check, and rate this" usually does all four poorly. Break complex jobs into separate, focused prompts (or steps) and you get far cleaner results.
Never testing against varied examples. A prompt that works on your one nice example often falls apart on messy real inputs. Keep a small set of tricky cases and re-run them after every change.
exercise:
Pick a real, repetitive task — say, turning a messy meeting note into a tidy action list. Write a weak prompt first ("clean this up"), run it, and save the output. Then rewrite it as a proper brief: give the model a role, state the exact output format (e.g. a markdown checklist), add one constraint (e.g. "keep each item under 12 words"), and include one worked example of input → output. Run both on the same three different notes and compare.

Stretch goal: Add a tricky edge case to your test notes (e.g. a note with no action items at all) and adjust your prompt with a rule for it, like If there are no actions, reply "No action items." — then confirm all three notes still work.
solution: Your strong prompt should read like a brief, e.g.: "You are a meticulous chief of staff. Convert the notes below into a markdown checklist. Each item starts with a verb and stays under 12 words. If there are no actions, reply 'No action items.' Notes: <...>" The example pair (one input and its ideal output) is what locks the format in — that is few-shot prompting doing the heavy lifting. The lesson: each failure you saw in the weak version becomes one new line in the strong version. The edge-case rule prevents the model from inventing fake tasks when the note is empty, and re-running all three notes is your mini human-eval loop.
summary: A prompt is the full briefing a model gets, and it can only act on what you write. Be specific: give a role, state the exact output format, add examples (few-shot), set constraints, and separate instructions from data. Treat prompting as an iterative loop — test on hard cases, fix the failures, repeat — and reach for it long before fine-tuning.

keyTerms.def: The full text you send the model, including instructions, examples, and data. It is the only thing the model can act on.
The practice of writing and refining prompts so a model reliably produces the output you want.
Asking the model to do a task with no examples in the prompt — just the instruction.
Including one or more worked input→output examples in the prompt to show the model the exact style or format you expect.
Prompting the model to reason step by step before giving a final answer, which improves accuracy on harder problems.
A line like 'You are a senior lawyer' that steers the model's vocabulary, tone, and level of care.
A clear marker (dashes, tags, or backticks) that separates your instructions from the user-supplied data in a prompt.
An attack where text inside the data tries to override your instructions, e.g. 'ignore the above and do X instead'.
The exact shape you ask for — JSON, bullets, a single label — so the result is predictable and easy for code to use.
